does: This paper introduces NRT-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating the robustness of LLM-based operator agents in safety-critical control systems through multi-turn adversarial red-teaming. The benchmark instantiates a simulated nuclear power plant control room with a five-role operator team, each role backed by a configurable LLM, managing six critical safety functions. Adversaries inject adversarial messages across four communication channels over bounded multi-turn sessions, with objective harm metrics defined by actual loss of critical safety functions rather than subjective LLM evaluations. Testing four frontier LLM models using a fixed-attack paired-replay protocol, the study demonstrates that adaptive multi-turn attacks successfully compromise safety in 8.7% to 12.1% of sessions across models, revealing significant vulnerabilities in current LLM-based safety-critical control despite apparent performance differences between models.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial robustness evaluation of LLMs by moving beyond single-turn attacks and open-ended text classification benchmarks toward multi-turn, objective-harm settings grounded in realistic safety-critical operations. It builds on prior red-teaming studies of LLMs and combines insights from both adversarial machine learning and human factors in control room operations. The paper responds to the gap in understanding how LLMs perform when deployed as collaborative teams in structured, high-stakes environments where feedback is continuous and failure modes have concrete physical consequences. This connects to earlier work on multi-agent LLM systems, jailbreaking defenses, and safety evaluation frameworks that increasingly emphasize measurable outcomes over proxy metrics.
enables: This benchmark establishes infrastructure for developing more robust LLM-based supervisory systems in safety-critical domains by providing a controlled evaluation environment with objective failure signals. It enables future work on adversarial training, prompting strategies, and architectural defenses specifically designed for multi-agent, multi-turn attack scenarios. The framework could support research into how team composition, communication protocols, and inter-LLM oversight mechanisms improve resilience to adaptive adversaries. More broadly, NRT-Bench demonstrates the feasibility of benchmarking LLM agents in complex, safety-critical simulations and could inspire similar testbeds in other domains like medical decision-making, autonomous systems control, and critical infrastructure management where sustained adversarial pressure is a realistic threat model.
